coredata.dc:title: Electron transfer and cascade relaxation dynamics of graphene quantum dots/MoS2 monolayer mixed-dimensional van der Waals heterostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Shan, Hangyong
Yu, Ying
Zhang, Rui
Cheng, Runtan
Zhang, Dong
Luo, Yang
Wang, Xingli
Li, Bowen
Zu, Shuai
Lin, Feng
Liu, Zheng
Chang, Kai
Fang, Zheyu
coredata.dc:description: Van der Waals (VdW) heterostructures have emerged as promising materials for atomically thin optoelectronic and photovoltaic applications, where the efficient charge separation after photo-excitation is significant to enhance device performances. However, modulating the interfacial charge transfer is still challenging due to the weak interlayer VdW interaction. Revealing electron transfer and relaxation processes in heterostructures lays foundation to tune the interfacial dynamics and optoelectronic properties. Here, we realize the efficient modulation of relaxation channels in MoS2 monolayers and interfacial electron transfer by forming mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). With femtosecond pump–probe spectroscopy, the biphasic electron injection model and modified rate equations are employed to quantitatively solve interfacial transfer rates of mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures. We find that the cascaded relaxation of hot electrons in GQDs that originates from the quantum confinement effect can intensively affect the interfacial dynamics. Our established model is instructive to optimize performances of future photon-harvesting devices based on mixed low-dimensional heterostructures.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of GQDs and MoS2 monolayers
Photo-excited electron relaxation dynamics of MoS2 monolayers
Differential reflection signals of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures
Electron transfer and relaxation dynamics of heterostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of GQDs and MoS2 monolayers. (a) Schematic of ultrafast pump–probe measurements in reflection configuration. The 400-nm pulse pumps GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures, and the 650-nm probe arrives at the sample with a delay time of Δτ. (b) The TEM image of GQDs, which shows an average diameter of 5 nm. The inset shows the size distribution of GQDs. (c) The reflection mapping of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures. The uniform green area demonstrates the homogenous distribution of GQDs on the MoS2 monolayers. (d) Absorption spectra of MoS2 monolayers and GQDs. The dashed line denotes the probe wavelength.

Differential reflection signals of the MoS2 monolayers and GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures. The pump and probe wavelengths are fixed at 400 and 650 nm, respectively. (a) Differential reflection of the pristine MoS2 monolayers and all-sized GQDs. The solid line is the fitting curve with the solution of Eq. (1). (b) The rising edge and decay process of the MoS2 monolayers on the first two picoseconds. Two decay steps with different rates are denoted as dashed lines. (c) Differential reflection signals of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures with the GQD size changed from 70 to 5 nm. The solid lines are fitting curves with the solution of Eq. (2). (d) Relaxation dynamics of the GQDs/MoS2 heterostructure with the dot size of 10 nm on the first ten picoseconds. The later intensity increase implies the appearance of a new channel that can inject electrons into the MoS2 monolayers. The inset shows peak intensities of ΔR/R 0(t) for the pristine MoS2 monolayers and all heterostructures.

The band diagram of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. The electron transfer and relaxation channels are depicted based on the biphasic electron injection model. The left panel denotes the band diagram of MoS2, the middle and right panels show calculated eigen states and DOS for 5-nm GQDs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Electron transfer and cascade relaxation dynamics of graphene quantum dots/MoS2 monolayer mixed-dimensional van der Waals heterostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, State Key Laboratory for Mesoscopic Physics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Collaborative Innovation Center of Quantum Matter, Peking University, Beijing 100871, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Mesoscopic Physics
Collaborative Innovation Center of Quantum Matter

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SKLSM, Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100083, China
School of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CNRS International-NTU-Thales Research Alliance (CINTRA), Nanyang Technological University, Singapore 637553, Singapore

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: H.Y.S., Y.Y. and R.Z. contributed equally to this work. Z.Y.F. supervised the project. H.Y.S. and Y.Y. performed experiments and established the theoretical model. R.Z. carried out energy level and DOS calculations. All authors discussed the results and wrote the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We realize the efficient modulation of relaxation channels in MoS2 monolayers and interfacial electron transfer by forming mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and find that the cascaded relaxation of hot electrons in GQDs that originate from the quantum confinement effect is the ultimate reason. With femtosecond pump–probe spectroscopy, the biphasic electron injection model and modified rate equations are employed to quantitatively solve the interfacial dynamics.

Van der Waals (VdW) heterostructures have emerged as promising materials for atomically thin optoelectronic and photovoltaic applications, where the efficient charge separation after photo-excitation is significant to enhance device performances. However, modulating the interfacial charge transfer is still challenging due to the weak interlayer VdW interaction. Revealing electron transfer and relaxation processes in heterostructures lays foundation to tune the interfacial dynamics and optoelectronic properties. Here, we realize the efficient modulation of relaxation channels in MoS2 monolayers and interfacial electron transfer by forming mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). With femtosecond pump–probe spectroscopy, the biphasic electron injection model and modified rate equations are employed to quantitatively solve interfacial transfer rates of mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures. We find that the cascaded relaxation of hot electrons in GQDs that originates from the quantum confinement effect can intensively affect the interfacial dynamics. Our established model is instructive to optimize performances of future photon-harvesting devices based on mixed low-dimensional heterostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) monolayers have attracted increasing attention for applications of photovoltaics [1,2], photodetection [3,4],catalysis [5], etc. Despite being atomically thin, many TMD monolayers are direct band-gap semiconductors with strong light–matter interactions, and their emission can cover a broad spectral range from visible to near-infrared [2,6–8]. Without conventional ‘lattice mismatch’ issues, it is easy to assemble different TMDs into vertical van der Waals (VdW) heterostructures [1,9], which further opens an avenue for the fundamental material study and optoelectronic device applications [1–3,10–12]. Interesting physics phenomena based on these two-dimensional (2D) heterostructures were investigated, such as Moiré superlattices, long lifetime interlayer excitons, and gate tunable band-gaps, which have been used to generate unique device functionalities in comparison with conventional semiconductors. For devices based on 2D heterostructures, the external quantum efficiency is usually suppressed by the back electron transfer and the charge recombination at interface. Thus, to understand the interfacial process of 2D heterostructures is essential for the future optoelectronic device design and applications [13–20].

On the other side, different dimensional materials can show various properties, e.g. quantum dots act as electron donors and sensitizers, while 2D crystals are potential active layers for the optoelectronic application [3,21–23]. Because the VdW interaction can exist at any passivated surfaces without dangling bonds, mixed-dimensional heterostructures can be obtained by stacking zero- and one-dimensional (0D and 1D) nanomaterials on TMD monolayers. The constructed 0D/2D and 1D/2D heterostructures can exhibit exceptional properties compared to 2D/2D VdW stackers [21,22,24], where the doping efficiency, carrier concentration, and interfacial charge transfer processes may be modulated with the abrupt change of energy level and density of states (DOS) in mixed-dimensional heterostructures. It is expected that VdW heterostructures constituted by mixed-dimensional materials can fully utilize their original synergetic effects to achieve a superior performance.

In this work, we investigate the electron transfer and cascade relaxation dynamics of the mixed-dimensional heterostructures comprised of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and MoS2 monolayers. GQDs serve as electron donors, while the MoS2 behaves like an acceptor. By decreasing the GQD size from 70 to 5 nm, the 2D/2D graphene flake-MoS2 heterostructure gradually becomes a 0D/2D GQDs–MoS2 stacker. The interfacial dynamics of different mixed-dimensional heterostructures is studied by employing femtosecond pump–probe measurements, and transient differential reflection signals are found dependent with the GQD size. The biphasic electron injection model is applied to elucidate the experimental observation, and different characteristic electron transfer rates at the 0D/2D interface are calculated and further explained by the Marcus theory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of GQDs and MoS2 monolayers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a is the schematic of our femtosecond pump–probe measurements, where the 400-nm pump pulse is generated by focusing the femtosecond laser (800 nm) into a BBO crystal. A portion of fundamental pulse is concentrated into a photonic crystal fiber to generate a super-continuum white light source with the wavelength extending from 550 to 1400 nm. The 650-nm probe pulse is selected from the white light with a band-pass filter. Compared with the pump, the probe pulse arrives at the sample with a delay time of Δτ, and the transient reflected probe light is recorded by the photomultiplier. Before arriving at the photomultiplier, the pump laser is filtered with a 450-nm-longpass filter (see more details in Materials and Methods).

The size of GQDs used in the experiments can be well characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, as shown in Fig. 1b. The inset shows the size distribution of GQDs, where an average diameter of 5 nm can be clearly distinguished. Other sized GQDs were imaged and are shown in Figs. S1a–S1c, with the corresponding size distributions exhibited in Figs. S1d–S1f. The monolayer property of MoS2 was certified by Raman spectra shown in Fig. S1g. The reflection mapping of 70 nm GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures is illustrated in Fig. 1c. The uniform green area indicates that GQDs are homogeneously deposited on top of MoS2 monolayers. Furthermore, specific absorption spectra at representative locations are plotted in Figs. S1h and S1i, which are similar due to the homogeneous distribution of GQDs (see more details in Note. S1). To quantitatively characterize the density of GQDs on top of MoS2, heterostructures with varied GQD sizes were scanned by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The AFM images are shown in Figs. S1j and S1k, from which individual GQDs can be clearly distinguished. The number of GQDs was counted statistically and the density was then calculated (Fig. S1l). A similar density was obtained because the GQD concentration (0.1 mg/mL) was same for different sized GQDs. Absorption spectra of the MoS2 monolayers and GQDs are shown in Fig. 1d, where the A and B excitons of MoS2 monolayers are recorded at 1.84 and 1.98 eV, while the GQDs show a broad absorption spectrum. Absorption spectra of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures with different GQD sizes and concentrations can be found in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-excited electron relaxation dynamics of MoS2 monolayers
ce:section.ce:para: The measured differential reflection signals ΔR/R 0(t) of the MoS2 monolayers and GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 a. ΔR/R 0(t) represents the transient relative reflection change of the probe pulse, equaling to (R − R 0)/R 0, where R and R 0 are reflections of the probe light with and without pump pulse. In our ultrafast measurements, in order to excite both the MoS2 monolayers and the GQDs, the wavelength of pump pulse was fixed at 400 nm, while the probe was chosen as 650 nm. The pump and probe fluences were 7.5 and 0.75 μJ/cm2, respectively. In Fig. 2a, the transient reflection signals for all-sized GQDs are at the base line, which demonstrates that the recorded transient absorption signal is only from the MoS2 monolayers, and the measured ΔR/R 0(t) is determined by the electron occupancy number at MoS2 exciton level.

The relaxation dynamics of pristine MoS2 monolayers is first analyzed from the differential reflection ΔR/R 0(t) in Fig. 2a. The relaxation of MoS2 monolayers on the first two picoseconds is extended and shown in Fig. 2b, where two processes with different rates can be clearly observed and the dominant decay process converts at the delay time about 1 ps. With the pump pulse, electrons of the MoS2 monolayers are excited and relaxed into the exciton level, which occurs on the rising edge of ΔR/R 0(t). These electrons that are accumulated at the exciton level then experience a fast decay process during the first hundreds of femtoseconds, which may be attributed to the annihilation of biexcitons or the thermalization of photo-excited carriers [25,26]. After this fast decay, the excited electrons undergo the exciton–exciton annihilation process.

In order to quantitatively study the electron relaxation dynamics, the relationship between the amplitude of measured ΔR/R 0(t) and the photo-excited electron density N should be established first. In the experiments, the peak amplitude of ΔR/R 0(t) for the pristine MoS2 monolayers is found linearly changed with the pump fluence, as shown in Fig. S3. Since the photo-excited electron density N is directly proportional to the pump fluence, it is safe to assume that the amplitude of ΔR/R 0(t) also varies linearly with N when its maximal amplitude is lower than 0.65% (Fig. S3) [27–29].

With this linear relationship, the rate equation for the electron relaxation that involves two decay processes can be presented as the following:
(1)
dN
dt
= - A
N x
-
1 2
γ
N 2
where A and x are coefficients, and γ/2 is the exciton–exciton annihilation rate (see Fig. S4 and Note. S2 for detailed derivations). In Eq. (1), the first item represents the fast decay process, while the second one corresponds to the exciton–exciton interaction. Based on the Monte Carlo method, these parameters can be obtained, where A is 0.41 × 10−24 cm6/s, x is 4, and γ/2 equals to 0.076 cm2/s. The obtained exciton–exciton annihilation rate is almost the same as previous reports [27,30]. With these parameters, fitting curves in Fig. 2a and b are plotted, which have a good agreement with experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential reflection signals of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The electron transfer and cascade relaxation dynamics of GQDs/MoS2 mixed-dimensional heterostructures was further analyzed with the ultrafast pump–probe measurements. In the experiments, the concentration of GQDs was found to hardly affect the relaxation dynamics of heterostructures (Fig. S5 and Note. S3), and it was fixed at 0.1 mg/mL following. In the measurements of heterostructures, the pump and probe wavelengths were fixed at 400 and 650 nm, respectively, with the same fluences as used for MoS2 monolayers. Fig. 2c shows the size-dependent differential reflection signals of GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures. Two-step relaxation processes can still be seen when the GQD size is larger than 40 nm. For the heterostructures with 70 nm GQDs, the effect of probe wavelength on the relaxation dynamics was studied and shown in Fig. S6. As the probe wavelength is gradually changed from 650 to 800 nm, the maximal intensity of ΔR/R 0(t) is decreased, while the relaxation curve still involves two distinct decay processes. This indicates that the change of probe wavelength can affect the amplitude of ΔR/R 0(t) but cannot dramatically influence the relaxation dynamics of heterostructures.

However, the differential reflection signal exhibits obvious distinctions when the GQD size decreases to 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 2c and d. The ultrafast signal promptly drops to the base line within the first hundreds of femtoseconds and then slightly increases with the delay time. Since the amplitude of ΔR/R 0(t) represents the photo-excited electron density, the dramatically reduced intensity implies the emergence of new channels that can dissipate photo-excited electrons in the MoS2. On the other side, the later intensity rising trend manifests the appearance of another channel that can inject electrons into the MoS2. When the GQD size reduces to 5 nm, the differential reflection also shows a single-step decay but without the later amplitude rising. These results demonstrate the relaxation dynamics of MoS2 is intensely altered when the 2D/2D heterostructures change to 0D/2D ones.

The inset of Fig. 2d shows the peak values of ΔR/R 0(t) for heterostructures. All measured amplitudes of heterostructures are lower than that of the pristine MoS2 monolayers. For heterostructures, the incident fluence is decreased when the pump pulse reaches the MoS2, because a portion of light is absorbed by the top GQDs. To estimate the influence of this effect, the absolute absorbance of different sized GQDs were measured as shown in Fig. S7, which exhibits a similar strength for different sized GQDs. However, it is interesting to note that the peak amplitudes of heterostructures are decreased with different extents for various sized GQDs. This result implies that the light absorption of top GQDs is not the exclusive reason that causes the reduction in peak amplitudes. These remarkable changes of ΔR/R 0(t) suggest a revised rate equation model is required to describe the dynamical behavior of mixed-dimensional heterostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transfer and relaxation dynamics of heterostructures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 depicts the biphasic electron injection model [14,31,32] that is used to explain the GQD size-dependent relaxation dynamics of the heterostructures. The left panel shows the energy diagram of MoS2 monolayers, and the middle one exhibits energy levels of 5-nm GQDs, which were calculated using the tight-binding approximation method (Note. S4). Four main processes involving the hot electron injection, hot electron cooling, cold electron injection, and back electron transfer are introduced in this model.

During the rising edge of differential reflection signals ΔR/R 0(t) for GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures, electrons in the MoS2 valence band are excited with the 400-nm pump laser and then rapidly decay into the exciton level, which is the same as the procedure occured in pristine MoS2 monolayers. Simultaneously, GQDs are also pumped and a portion of photo-excited electrons can inject into the MoS2, corresponding to the process of hot electron injection, shown as the red arrow in Fig. 3. The injected electrons can participate in the generation of MoS2 trions (comprised of two electrons and one hole) [33], which decreases the electron density of MoS2 at exciton level and further weakens the differential reflection amplitude (inset of Fig. 2d).

Other main processes are involved in the descent process of ΔR/R 0(t). For those hot electrons retained in the GQDs, they can be non-radiatively cooled to lower states and become cold electrons, shown as black arrows in Fig. 3. The lifetime of this cooling process is seriously dependent on the GQD size. As the dot size decreased from 70 to 5 nm, the quantum confinement effect gradually emerges. To ascertain this quantum effect, DOS of different sized GQDs were calculated and are shown in Fig. S8. For large-sized GQDs, the calculated DOS is almost continuous, but apparent energy gaps appear when the GQD size decreases to 5 nm, as plotted in the right panel of Fig. 3. For small-sized GQDs, the spacing of discrete energy levels can even exceed the highest phonon energy of GQDs. The ‘phonon bottleneck’ indicates that multi phonons are requested to participate in the hot electron cooling process [34], which can slow the cooling time even up to two orders [35].

With such a longer lifetime, cold electrons can occupy higher energy levels of the GQDs and gradually cascade to lower states rather than straightly jump to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). When the occupied level of cold electrons is higher than the MoS2 conduction band edge, the cold electron injection from GQDs to the MoS2 can occur, shown as the blue arrow in Fig. 3.

These injected electrons can further generate excitons in the MoS2 with redundant holes that are left by the back electron transfer process, in which electrons are reversely migrated from the MoS2 to top GQDs. This back transfer process can be inferred by comparing the descent procedures of differential reflection signals in Fig. 2, where the amplitude of mixed-dimensional heterostructures decreases more rapidly in comparison with the one of pristine MoS2 monolayers. The more rapid decay of ΔR/R 0(t) for heterostructures implies the emergence of a new channel in the MoS2 (the back electron transfer) that can dissipate the electron density at the exciton level.

A recent literature studied the interfacial electron transfer dynamics of MoS2/WSe2 2D VdW heterostructures theoretically with time-dependent density functional theory [36]. This work found an interfacial relaxation pathway of hot electrons, of which the electron transfer rate is much faster than the intralayer relaxation rate and also reported the appearance of hot electron back transfer. It can strongly support the biphasic electron injection model and provides evidences to indicate that this electron transfer model can be universal in VdW heterostructures with suitable energy band alignments.

With above discussions, we can see both the cold electron injection and back electron transfer processes should be considered in the revised rate equation for the heterostructures. Based on the relaxation equation of pristine MoS2 monolayers (Eq. (1)), the modified rate equation set for heterostructures can be written as the following:
(2)
dN
dt
= - A
N 4
-
1 2
γ
N 2
-
N
τ
back
+
M
τ
cold
dM
dt
= -
M
τ
cold
+ G
G =
M 0
τ
cooling
where τback, τcold, and τcooling are lifetimes of back electron transfer, cold electron injection, and hot electron cooling, respectively; N is the electron density of MoS2 at exciton level, G is the cold electron generation rate, M 0 represents the original density of retained hot electrons in GQDs, and M is the electron density at M level of GQDs. Because the cold electron injection rate is different at varied energy levels of GQDs and is seriously influenced by the electronic DOS distribution, an average rate at a single energy level (M level as shown in Fig. 3) is modeled to represent the whole process of cold electron injection from GQDs to the MoS2.

Because the cooling time for hot electrons is on the order of picosecond, which is much longer than the hot electron injection, it is safe to consider the hot electron cooling process starts after the hot electron injection. Hence, the initial quantity for M in Eq. (2) can be considered as zero. By settling initial conditions of N and M 0, the rate equation set can be fully solved using the Monte Carlo method (see Notes. S5 and S6 for more details).

The lifetime of different processes are fitted and plotted in Fig. 4 . The τcooling grows as the decrease in GQD size (Fig. 4a). This can be explained by the quantum confinement effect mentioned above, and the fitted cooling time is on the order of 10 ps, consisting well with previous literatures [35,37]. The result illustrated in the inset of Fig. 2d, which demonstrates the enhanced hot electron injection density for small-sized GQDs, can be also aroused by the quantum effect. The sum density of electrons that undergo the hot electron injection and hot electron cooling is fixed due to the same pump fluence, implying that these two channels are competitive. For small-sized GQDs, their cooling lifetimes are prolonged due to the phonon bottleneck. Therefore, the reduced hot electron cooling rate makes more hot electrons injecting into the MoS2.

Fig. 4b shows that both τcold and τback decrease with the reduction in GQD size. This can be explained by the Marcus theory, which indicates that the electron transfer rate is basically determined by the driving force, DOS in acceptor, electronic coupling, reorganization energy and Fermi occupancy factor [14,31,38]. The cold electron injection rate (reciprocal of the lifetime 1/τcold ) is dramatically reduced with the increment of GQD size and can be finally ignored when the 0D/2D GQDs–MoS2 heterostructure changes to the 2D/2D type. For small-sized GQDs, because of the longer cooling time, cascaded cold electrons can occupy a higher energy level, where the larger DOS distribution in the corresponding MoS2 monolayers makes the cold electron injection more easily.

For the back electron transfer, the calculated rate (1/τback ) is also decreased with the increase in GQD size, as shown in Fig. 4b. This is because the retained hot electrons in GQDs can faster cool to lower energy levels when the GQD has a large size and can be taken as a graphene flake. This electron jumping effect in relaxation procedure makes energy levels near the LUMO rapidly occupied, and thus the back electron transfer is less likely to occur.

With parameters illustrated in Fig. 4, the delay time-dependent photo-excited electron densities N(t) for all-sized GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures can be numerically solved from Eq. (2). Solid lines in Fig. 2c and d are fitting curves derived from N(t), which agree well with the experimental results. The ΔR/R 0(t) signals of heterostructures in Fig. 2c are determined by the competition result between the channel that increases the electron density (cold electron injection
M
τ
cold
) and channels that dissipate the density (back electron transfer
-
N
τ
back
and intrinsic relaxations
- A
N 4
-
1 2
γ
N 2
). The interfacial dynamics of 2D/2D heterostructures (GQD size larger than 40 nm) is almost the same as the pristine MoS2 monolayers, because both the cold and back electron transfers act as perturbations compared to the intrinsic relaxation channels. The small deviation between the numerical solution and experimental data in Fig. 2c is attributed to fluctuations of intrinsic relaxations in MoS2 monolayers (see more details in Note. S6). Although all items in Eq. (2) can affect transient signals due to the correlated N and M, the rising trend for 10-nm GQDs is induced by the dramatically increased cold electron injection rate. As to heterostructures with 5-nm GQDs, the absence of later amplitude rising trend is primarily ascribed to the enhanced back electron transfer rate.

In conclusion, the electron transfer and cascade relaxation dynamics of GQDs/MoS2 VdW heterostructures was investigated with the femtosecond pump–probe spectroscopy. We revealed that the relaxation channels of photo-excited electrons in MoS2 monolayers and interfacial transfer rates can be efficiently modulated by controlling the size of GQDs. With the biphasic electron injection model, modified relaxation rate equations were employed to quantitatively solve the dynamical behavior of mixed-dimensional heterostructures, where all of the rate parameters can be elucidated by the Marcus theory. Our proposed 0D/2D VdW heterostructures offer an effective method for tuning the relaxation dynamics of 2D materials. An ultrafast and efficient charge separation is a crucial step in collecting photo-excited charges for optoelectronic and photovoltaic devices. Our established electron transfer model is instructive to optimize interfacial processes and to improve performances of the future photon-conversion devices based on mixed-dimensional VdW heterostructures.

The MoS2 monolayers were synthesized by the chemical vapor deposition method at 850 °C, with MoO3 and pure sulfur as reactant materials [39]. The substrate was composed of Si/SiO2 with SiO2 thickness in 540 nm. GQDs were synthesized from anthracite (Fisher Scientific, catalog number S98806) and bituminous coal (Fisher Scientific, catalog number S98809). Detailed synthesis and filtration processes can be found in the reference [40]. Different sized GQDs solutions were first prepared by dissolving the powder of GQDs into DI water and then spin-coated onto the MoS2 monolayers. The prepared sample was put on a 100 °C hot plate to evaporate the solvent and ensure intimate contact.

The relative change of reflection was used to characterize sample absorption spectrum. The relative reflection was acquired by dividing the reflection of the sample to that of the substrate. Reflection spectra were measured with the hyperspectral imaging system (Cytoviva HIS V3) and were recorded by the spectrometer (Horiba iHR550) with a 40× objective lens (Olympus LUCPlanFL N, NA = 0.6).

Ultrafast pump–probe measurements in reflection configuration were carried out. Detailed descriptions of our home-built apparatus can be found in a previous work [41]. The mode-locked oscillator (Tsunami 3941C-25XP) with a repetition rate 80 MHz and a pulse duration of ∼ 73 fs was used. The output 800-nm femtosecond pulse was split into two parts. One of them passed through a BBO crystal to produce the 400-nm pump, while the other one was focused into a photonic crystal fiber (Newport SCG-800) to generate the super-continuum white light. A mechanical chopper with 1500 Hz was utilized to modulate the pump. The delay time between pump and probe pulses was controlled by a steeper linear stage (Newport M-ILS150PP). The spot size of the focused probe was about 2 µm, and only those locations with similar homogeneously distributed GQDs were measured. The detailed analysis of pump and probe pulse durations is shown in Fig. S9 and Note. S7.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.
The raw data required to reproduce these findings are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant no. 2017YFA0205700), National Basic Research Program of China (grant no. 2015CB932403, 2017YFA0206000, 2017YFA0303400), National Science Foundation of China (grant nos. 11674012, 61521004, 21790364, 61422501 and 11374023), Beijing Natural Science Foundation (grant nos. Z180011 and L140007), Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China (grant no. 201420), and National Program for Support of Top-notch Young Professionals (grant no.W02070003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mattod.2019.01.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
